I have a Polestar 3 Performance with Plus, Pilot, Nappa and HD lights. My delivery experience was very poor (arrived with lots of scratches and key fob not sync'd to the car). I did kick up a fuss and to be fair the dealer responsible for PDI did step up, collected my car, provided another PS 3 loan car and returned it within the expected time. I was also given a Electroverse charge credit. The Polestar customer service rep kept in touch throughout.Since then it "mostly" works really well. My issues have been fairly minor, the connection with CarPlay doesn't automatically connect, sometimes it has no mobile data connection (it usually comes back after a few minutes) and a couple of times it has lost its GPS location. This is despite the latest software update, which is meant to address these. Hopefully the next will continue to improve stability. I had similar issues with my previous Telsa a few years ago, when their platform was "less stable" than it is now.But I've had no issues with charging or locking/unlocking the car (whether by key fob or digital key on my phone).It is supremely comfortable to drive, is very quiet, handles very well indeed on a windy road and the build quality and materials are first rate, it is a very special place to sit. Much, much more premium than our last Model Y Performance and not a lot slower.Economy is "ok" for something so large/heavy. I can get over 3 miles/kwh on long motorway trips, doing 60-65mph. So easily more than 300 miles, if a bit less than the 350 mile claim. Mine has 22" wheels, the 20" wheel version can do 50 miles more (and has a slightly better ride).The B&W audio is amazing (part of the Plus pack), best I've ever had in a car by a long way, especially on well recorded/mixed Dolby Atmos content (try REM's Automatic for the People or Dire Straits, Brothers in Arms).Some people seem to have had major issues but I've done nearly 4000 miles without anything that would want me to give it back.

There was a generation of attempts at EVs in the 80s/90s with lead acid or NiMH batteries and DC motors. They were fine, but not great. They didn't have much range, didn't have thermally managed batteries or fast charging, and didn't have the torque and power we expect from a modern AC motor electric vehicle. The batteries were also very expensive, so most of them were only sold to meet emissions requirements and would have lost money as standalone commercial products. Think of them more like scaled-up golf carts, which is where some of the lingering opinions about EVs come from. The modern electric vehicle came around when lithium ion batteries and AC inverter driven motors became mass-market ready and fixed pretty much all of those early electric vehicle issues.

Are we seeing the beginning of the third generation of modern EVs? I feel like the first generation spanned from the limited-production cars like the EV1 up through Nissan Leaf and other <100mi range early cars Gen 2 starts very clearly for me with the Model S, and includes evolutions and improvements through today. Are we on the precipice of Gen 3? Arguably, the Cybertruck might be the first Gen 3 electric vehicle, given the significant architectural steps. (Not expressing an opinion about its design!) I’m thinking my next electric vehicle might be either a Rivian R2, which feels to me like a final evolution of Gen 2, or a BMW iX3, which feels more like an early Gen 3. Is this a valid take?

You need to define the endpoints. You could definitely say that L3 fast charging capabilities is Gen 2. But there's no line of demarcation for Gen 3. It could be 10-minute fast charging. It could be getting off Lithium batteries. It could be evolving past the requirements of competing with Tesla on automated driving, software-driven everything, and so on. It could be actual FSD, which has nothing to do with being an electric vehicle except an electric vehicle will do it first. So far I see we're still in an evolutionary time period without a clear line.

There is lots of evidence that it is NMC. The voltage curve is a really strong evidence, it perfectly fits NMC and that would be very hard to fabricate with other chemistries (especially when you claim it is non-lithium). The internal resistance at the high power charge test is exactly what you'd expect from a high energy NMC cell. The fact that the cell puffed at the high temperature test is also a very strong indicator that it is a liquid electrolyte, not solid state. Also the fact that Donut failed to deliver any proof for anything you wouldn't be able to do with a NMC cell while still claiming otherwise basically confirms the suspicion that they just want to sell a standard NMC cell they didn't even build themselves as a new chemistry.

Which version and trim of the Ariya did you buy? I’m assuming you bought the small battery pack variant…the Engage? The way to go would have been the following trims: Venture+ Engage+ Evolve+ Empower+ Platinum+ These larger battery pack trims would have given you significantly more range out of a single charge. We regularly road trip our 2024 Ariya Evolve+ e-4orce from Los Angeles to Las Vegas without issues. That’s 250 miles easily covered, although we do stop for lunch in Barstow and plug it in (because, why not we’re already stopped). In regards to your Tesla adapter not working…keep in mind that not all Tesla supercharger locations are open to non-Tesla EVs. You have to use the Tesla app and filter out compatible stations with your vehicle. And make sure you are using the correct adapter for the right charging situation. There are two types: One is J1772 to NACS- this is used exclusively for AC level 2 charging. The other type is CCS1 to NACS - this is exclusively for DC fast charging. These adapters are not interchangeable. Once you live with the Ariya for a bit, and understand how it works, the experience will be a lot less stressful. Unfortunately, you took the car right out on a road trip without first understanding how EVs behave in terms of efficiency during sustained high speed trips. Wishing you many years of happy electric vehicle driving!

I think the sentiment is that experienced electric vehicle drivers understand stuff. For example, that the displayed range in miles is a sheer guess, and a guess without a clue about the future (no time travel). So, no idea about where and how fast you will drive today. And if you really want an accurate range estimate, you should tell the car where you are going (via Nav). Also, if it is warm (80F) and you drive slow (20mph) around town, your range will be way higher than the EPA estimate (maybe double). But if it is cold (20F) and you drive fast (80mph) your range will be way lower than the EPA estimate (could be half). And, this is all true for gasoline vehicles too.

The 'rule of thumb' for SC varieties : * If it is a 'magic dock', no adapter required, but pretty rare 'in the wild'. * If it is a V4 charger (big white monolith, no central hole, 325 KW rating), you're good with a quality CCS1 to NACS adapter, tesla app set up. Cable is long enough to reach most plug point, but you need to park pretty close to the curb for back-mounts. The side mounts are easy. * If it is a V3 (center hole with the cable inside it, typically 250 KW rating), you should be able to charge if you can get the cable to reach. The 'back mount' are a PIA, 'side mount' are easier. If you do parking gyrations to access (e.g. block 2 towers), and it is busy, you'll get the stink eye - or cussed out. * If it is a V2, Tesla only. Center hole, 150KW rating. * If you're curious, here's [a map site for SC installations.]( Click on the site, it gives the charger details for the site. * Tesla app gives the correct listing for 'non-Tesla vehicle accessible sites in the 'charge my other electric vehicle' menu choice. * Supposedly, ABRP also filters correctly if you check 'NACS' as an allowable plug for the non-Tesla vehicle. * If you have a 800V car (e.g. Hyundai Ioniq 5), only some of V4 towers will give good charging performance. Most others will be S L O W because of how Hyundai works with 400V chargers. Typically pull-through ones will be 'belter' because they're 'CyberTruck targeted', which is the only Tesla with >400V charging. You may also get the 'stink eye' when not towing, because that's what those towers are targeting.

Indeed, most of the ones here in STL are Tesla only, I think you can count the open ones on one hand with fingers to spare. I really do not rely on Tesla even though the CCS situation isn't much better here.

As far as the slow charger in Vegas, put simply, it was probably just broken. One of the locals there told me "that's the slow one" once I'd been hooked up for a while, even though it had the same 350 kW labeling as the others. As for the Tesla charger... apparently China got me, and I bought an adapter that isn't compatible with 99% of vehicles, yet has 4.5 star reviews everywhere.

BZ 2026, reasons not to use max regenerative breaking? Reasons for using max regenerative breaking are: Increased range. Less wear on brakes. Reasons against: Coming from an internal combustion engine vehicle im accustomed to chasing and not losing much speed. Overall if someone is okay with the driving experience, seems you should always use max regen. Edit: My initial assertions were completely wrong. See thread.

>You will, even if it’s at a tiny scale, be oscillating between regen and acceleration, and wasting energy. Unless the manufacturer puts a deadband in the pedal mapping. Then it can be very easy to coast, even in a car with aggressive regen that only operates in one pedal driving mode.

So what happens if you're in ACC mode and you disengage? In my experience it is full regen and hard deceleration which is uncomfortable. To avoid this you have to know exact pedal position at that speed to not have any sudden change in speed. And tapping the brake will also disengage ACC and go into full regen. Edit: I know some EVs have variable modes of regen to make it smooth, but many do not. Some are also either barely any regen or full on hard regen. Mine has 4 levels. D1-3 and B mode. The $800 Pecron F3000 battery generator can give you Level 1 charging (120v/12a/1440w) off of solar. Maybe 15-20 miles a sunny day. You would need about 1600 watts or so of steady solar input. Cost of solar panels varys widely as does the definition of "portable". One advantage of using a generator is that it can also be used as a backup source of power. Or at least you can tell your significant other that when they complain about the cost. If you do go down this rabbit hole and start looking at generators then pay attention to the max solar DC voltage rating. The F3000 can take 120 volts while many others in the same category can only do 60 volts. 60 is not enough for safe level 1 charging.

The slowest charging option for my car is 1.7KW, of which 1.4KW ends up in the battery, and 300W is 'wasted' on the charging electronics and auxciliary systems. Unless a panel can deliver at least that much continuously, my car won't get charged. Your car may be different, but I wouldn't bet on it.

I believe 6A is the slowest option for any standard electric vehicle; at least that's what the type 1 and 2 charging port specs call for. So you should be able to charge at 1.4kW using 230V (with a net 1.1kW going to the battery.) In the USA where we use 120V, 6A gives us 720 watts, and only 600 watts with Japan's 100 VAC. Of course at low wattage, that 300 watt overhead is a killer! Nearly half the incoming power is wasted!

it is a 200W panel and a 1.5kWh LFP battery. That product makes zero sense. You're going to hurt your efficiency 100% of the time you drive because it is mounted on the roof. And assuming you park outside and have ideal conditions you'd get only 200W continuous (ideal... actually will be even less). To put that in perspective, 200W is less than many EVs consume just to run their computers. So you'd actually be LOSING range trying to charge off of a 200W panel. Now the point of the LFP battery, I guess, is so you can charge at a higher peak rate. Given the inverter specs it seems like they're implying 12A @ 120V -- very typical L1 speeds. However, given the small size of the battery you'd basically only be able to charge for ONE HOUR per day ONCE. And then you're done. The solar panel cannot keep up to recharge the battery, even in a whole day. The more I look at this product the less it makes sense. 100% scam.
